_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Toxins and Environmental: Opioid Poisoning/Overdose. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Toxins and Environmental: Opioid Poisoning/Overdose

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Toxins and Environmental: Opioid Poisoning/Overdose
points: Rapid recognition and intervention of a clinically significant opioid poisoning or overdose
Prevention of respiratory and/or cardiac arrest

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Patients exhibiting decreased mental status, and respiratory depression of all age groups with known or suspected opioid use or abuse. Lack of miosis (pinpoint pupils) is not a reliable sign for ruling out opioid exposure, although its presence is consistent with such exposure
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
Patients with altered mental status exclusively from other causes (e.g., head injury, or hypoglycemia)

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Remove patient from exposure; call for ALS backup
Oxygen: BVM with high-flow O2 for apneic patient; NRM for hypoxic breathing patient
Recovery position for breathing patient with decreased LOC
Naloxone intranasal unit-dose (premeasured auto-injector) 4 mg IN for suspected opioid-induced respiratory depression
CPR/AED if pulseless; apneic patients often respond to naloxone + BVM alone

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Naloxone 2-4 mg IN (repeat q 2-3 min) or 0.4-2 mg IM via auto-injector for respiratory depression
BVM ventilation to support oxygenation until naloxone takes effect
Supraglottic airway if unable to maintain airway manually
Blood glucose monitoring
12-lead ECG if prolonged QRS or suspected co-ingestion
CPAP not appropriate in opioid OD (patient may not protect airway); prioritize naloxone + BVM

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: Naloxone 0.4-2 mg IV/IO; titrate to adequate respirations — goal is to restore breathing, not to fully reverse (avoid acute withdrawal and re-sedation)
IV access initiation; IV normal saline for hypotension
Waveform capnography to monitor ventilatory adequacy
IV D50W 25 g if hypoglycemia contributing to AMS
Naloxone infusion consideration for long-acting opioids (fentanyl, methadone) — half of reversal dose/hour IV

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: Naloxone titrated: start 0.04 mg IV q 2 min to restore respirations — avoid abrupt full reversal in opioid-dependent patients (precipitates acute withdrawal, agitation)
Endotracheal intubation for persistent apnea not reversed by naloxone or when airway protection needed
Naloxone infusion: 2/3 of reversal dose per hour IV for long-acting opioids (methadone, sustained-release oxycodone) — monitor for re-narcotization
Consider co-ingestions: benzodiazepines (flumazenil), stimulants, alcohol — treat per toxicology protocols
Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mEq/kg IV for QRS >120 ms (suggests sodium channel toxicity from co-ingestion)
12-lead ECG interpretation: QT prolongation risk with methadone, fentanyl analogs
RSI and intubation for co-ingestion with airway compromise not responsive to naloxone

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Clinical duration of naloxone a. The clinical opioid reversal effect of naloxone is limited and may end within an hour whereas opioids often have a duration of 4 hours or longer

Opioid withdrawal a. Patients with altered mental status secondary to an opioid overdose may become agitated or violent following naloxone administration due to opioid withdrawal therefore the goal is to use the lowest dose as possible to avoid precipitating withdrawal

EMS clinicians should be prepared to initiate airway management before, during, and after naloxone administration and to provide appropriate airway support until the patient has adequate respiratory effort

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: The essential feature of opioid overdose requiring EMS intervention is respiratory depression or apnea, managed by ventilation followed by naloxone
Some opioids have additional toxic effects (i.e., methadone can produce QT prolongation and tramadol can produce seizures)
Overuse and abuse of prescribed and illegal opioids has led to an increase in accidental and intentional opioid overdoses
Opioid combinations:
High-potency opioids:
The IN route has the benefit of no risk of needle stick to the clinician

Patients with opioid overdose from fentanyl or fentanyl analogs may rapidly exhibit chest wall rigidity and require positive end expiratory pressure (PEEP), in addition to multiple and/or larger doses of naloxone, to achieve adequate ventilation
The primary clinical indication for the use of opioid medications is analgesia

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Rapid and accurate identification of signs and symptoms of opioid poisoning
' Airway management
' Pulse oximetry (oxygen saturation) and, if available, capnometry or capnography
' Blood glucose assessment
' Naloxone dose and route of administration
' Clinical response to medication administration
' Number of doses of naloxone to achieve a clinical response

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Contact medical direction for clinical deterioration or interventions outside standard scope
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
